Structure of High-Pressure 
Premixed Ammonia, Hydrogen, 
and Methane Flames With 
100 kHz OH-PLIF Measurements
The impact of fuel composition on flame dynamics is investigated at 10 bar using 100 kHz 
hydroxyl planar laser induced fluorescence (OH-PLIF) measurements. The PLIF images are 
synchronized with high-frequency pressure measurements at distinct locations in the 
chamber to investigate mechanisms for thermo-acoustic feedback with the flame heat release 
and resonant acoustic modes of the combustor. Experiments were conducted over a range of 
fuel compositions spanning a hydrogen fuel fraction (v) of 30% to 90%, and an ammonia 
decomposition efficiency (g) from 40% to 100%. In predominantly hydrogen-rich fuel 
mixtures (v, g > 0.8), flame stabilization occurs along the shear layer, and flame holding is 
observed closer to the burner face. As methane composition increases (v < 0.8), significant 
flame corrugation and commensurate high heat release rate are observed as chemical 
kinetic rates and thermodiffusive effects decrease. In these cases, OH radical production and 
transport between neighboring injector elements are observed. The flame heat release and 
transport of combustion products in this region occur during the compression phase of the 
acoustic cycle and are responsible for ignition of fresh reactants. These periodic ignition, 
heat release, and flame displacement at the resonant acoustic mode frequency of the 
combustor result in self-excited longitudinal combustion instabilities (p0
RMS=pCC  1–7%). 
With significant concentrations of methane and ammonia in the fuel blend (v < 0.9, g < 0.6), 
the amplitude of this instability increases (p0
RMS=pCC > 7%), and periodic global extinction 
and re-ignition of flames are observed with acoustic expansion and compression.  
[DOI: 10.1115/1.4069450] 
Keywords: combustion instability, hydrogen combustion, ammonia combustion, high- 
pressure flames, lean-premixed flames, OH-PLIF 
1 Introduction
Concerns of increasing global greenhouse gas emissions have 
sparked rapid development efforts in carbon-free combustion 
technology [1]. In stationary power generation, fuels such as 
hydrogen (H2) and ammonia (NH3) have potential as carbon-free 
alternatives to natural gas but present unique challenges that inhibit 
widespread utilization. Hydrogen has established methods for 
renewable production and possesses the highest gravimetric energy 
density of any fuel. However, its low volumetric energy density 
makes it prohibitive for efficient storage, transport, and distribution 
at an industrial scale. The availability and cost of hydrogen derived 
from carbon-free sources also remain challenging, especially at the 
scale necessary to replace natural gas. Hydrogen also presents 
several operability concerns as a drop-in alternative to natural gas. 
The flame speed of hydrogen is an order of magnitude greater than 
that of methane, the primary constituent of natural gas. The large 
increase in kinetic timescale of hydrogen flames leads to an 
increased risk of flashback [2]. Ammonia, on the other hand, 
contains a larger density of hydrogen atoms than liquid hydrogen. 
Pre-existing facilities for the production, storage, and transport of 
ammonia can also reduce the economic complexity of the fuel’s 
large-scale implementation [3]. However, the slow kinetic rates of 
Turbo Expo: Turbomachinery Technical Conference & Exposition (GT2025), June 
16–20, 2025. GT2025.
1Corresponding author.
Manuscript received July 3, 2025; final manuscript received July 15, 2025; published 
online September 24, 2025. Editor: Jerzy T. Sawicki.
Journal of Engineering for Gas Turbines and Power                                                                                                                                      
DECEMBER 2025, Vol. 147 / 121018-1 
Copyright V
C 2025 by ASME
Downloaded from http://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/gasturbinespower/article-pdf/147/12/121018/7530910/gtp-25-1276.pdf by University of Cincinnati user on 31 December 2025

pure ammonia combustion limit the operating space of retrofit gas 
turbine combustors [4]. Moreover, the nitrogen atom present in the 
fuel introduces unique chemical pathways which can lead to high 
NOx emissions [5]. Blending of ammonia with hydrogen can 
mitigate undesirable properties of the individual fuels [6]. Never­
theless, carbon-free developments in combustor technology must 
accommodate these fuel blends with natural gas over a wide range of 
compositions to offset discrepancies in energy demand and fuel 
availability.
Lean-premixed combustion is common for low NOx natural gas- 
fired power generation gas turbine combustors. Successful retrofit­
ting of these combustors to operate with hydrogen and ammonia fuel 
blends requires understanding of the physical processes that govern 
the structure and propagation of the flame. Premixed hydrogen 
combustion has distinctly different underlying physics governing 
flame propagation compared to methane [7–10]. One such differ­
ence is the response to flame stretch, a phenomenon often described 
by the Markstein length. The Markstein length is a characteristic 
length scale that quantifies the sensitivity of local flame speed to 
flame curvature [11]. The Markstein number is a nondimensional 
parameter defined as the ratio of Markstein length to flame thickness. 
At lean conditions, Al-Khafaji et al. [12] reported a decrease in 
Markstein number and subsequent increase in stretch sensitivity 
with hydrogen addition to methane flames. For ammonia–hydrogen 
fuel blends, Ichikawa et al. [9] noted a nonmonotonic trend of 
Markstein length with variation in hydrogen mole fraction. The 
effect of hydrogen addition in lean ammonia flames is less 
understood, especially at engine relevant conditions. At elevated 
pressure, the operability space of these fuel mixtures has been 
characterized for several injector designs [4,13]. However, these 
studies are also scarce, and further experimental efforts at high 
pressure are necessary to resolve the fundamental processes 
affecting the structure and dynamics of these flames.
Despite the lack of empirical data, first principles analysis can still 
provide insight into the effect of hydrogen addition on the 
propagation of ammonia flames. Incommensurate rates of thermal 
and mass diffusion (nonunity Lewis number) have a prominent 
effect on flame stretch sensitivity [14]. When the Lewis number is 
less than one, typical of hydrogen combustion, mass diffusion 
occurs at a faster rate than thermal diffusion [15], leading to a 
thermodiffusive mechanism for intrinsic flame instabilities to 
develop [16]. In contrast, Lewis numbers closer to unity are typical 
for methane combustion, and preferential thermodiffusive insta­
bilities are less likely to occur. In premixed turbulent flames, this 
effect is believed to greatly increase the flame speed as turbulent 
intensity enhances wrinkling and leads to the development of 
smaller-scale structures in the flame [10,17,18]. A recent numerical 
study by Gaucherand et al. [8] investigated

an equivalence ratio uncertainty less than 2% of the mean. This 
duration is sufficient for the acquisition of steady, limit-cycle OH- 
PLIF and pressure measurements with negligible heat loss effects.
A parameterized expression of fuel composition is described in 
the following equation:

The hydrogen fuel used in the experiment is assumed to be 
derived from ammonia as a hydrogen carrier through a decom­
position process that yields a three to one molar ratio of hydrogen to 
nitrogen. The fraction of this mixture in the fuel is denoted by v, 
where v ¼ 0 corresponds to operation with only CH4 and v ¼ 1 
corresponds to operation with only H2/N2. The fraction of hydrogen 
to ammonia is denoted by a decomposition efficiency g. For all test 
cases reported, the maximum computed v uncertainty is 2.5%, and 
the maximum computed g uncertainty is 1.1%.
2.2 Diagnostics. Pressure fluctuations in the combustor were 
measured using recess mounted water-cooled high-frequency 
piezoresistive transducers (Kulite WCT312M-35/70BARA) at 
multiple axial locations in the combustor and sampled at 2.5 MHz 
[32]. The locations of the pressure transducers in the combustion 
chamber are marked with C1 and C2 in Fig. 1.
The OH-PLIF system used in this work is depicted in Fig. 2. The 
third harmonic (355 nm) from an Nd:YAG burst-mode laser is 
utilized to pump an optical parametric oscillator (OPO) in a double 
pass configuration (Spectral Energies QuasiModo, Beavercreak, 
OH). The OPO cavity is injection seeded with an 850 nm external 
cavity diode laser (Sacher, Lion, Marburg, Germany) to narrow the 
609 nm signal line-width. This signal is mixed with the second 
harmonic output of the burst-mode laser (532 nm) to produce a 
system output frequency of 284 nm to target the Q2ð8Þ line of the 
A2Rþð0 ¼ 1Þ  X2Pð00 ¼ 0Þ transition of OH. The system output 
pulse energy is 1 mJ/pulse.
The 284 nm beam is collimated to a 42 mm tall

title: School of Aeronautics and Astronautics,
text:
throughout the burst, only images from the first 2 ms of the laser 
burst are analyzed in the present study. The vertical dashed lines in 
Fig. 3(a) denote the beginning and end of the representative test 
case. Pixel columns are summed for each laser sheet image to 
produce an intensity distribution over the length of the sheet, which 
is depicted in Fig. 3(b) for a representative sheet image. All profiles 
from a single 2 ms laser burst are also plotted in Fig. 3(c), where 
slight variations in profile and intensity can be observed over the 
burst duration.
Characterization of spatial and shot-to-shot nonuniformity within 
the laser sheet profile is shown in Fig. 3. The sheet profile 
measurements allow for correction of the original images, as PLIF 
signal scales linearly with laser excitation energy. The 1D intensity 
profiles are normalized, scaled, and interpolated to match the 
resolution of the PLIF images. Pixel columns of the PLIF images are 
then divided by the normalized intensity values. To account for laser 
pulse energy decay over the imaging duration, individual frames are 
also divided by their corresponding integrated sheet intensity values, 
which are normalized by the maximum value for each test. A 
representative original OH-PLIF image is plotted alongside the 
corrected image in Fig. 4. The original image in Fig. 4(a) shows low 
intensity near the burner face, but features in the jet shear layers are 
restored in the corrected image in Fig. 4(b). Lower intensity regions 
downstream of the reactant jets are also amplified, yielding a more 
spatially uniform signal. Although this method of correction is 
accompanied with a discernible increase in noise, a more faithful 
interpretation of the images is possible as a result.
3 Results and Discussion
A summary of cases examined in this work is provided in Table 1. 
Three of the cases correspond to H2/CH4 fuel mixtures and are 
denoted by prefix M. The other cases consist of H2/NH3/CH4 ternary 
mixtu

title: School of Aeronautics and Astronautics,
text:
OH PLIF images enables the analysis of changes to pertinent 
flow–flame interactions with fuel composition.
3.1 Effect of Methane Addition. Within the flamelet regime, 
hydrogen enrichment of methane–air flames enhances turbulent 
burning velocity for consistent turbulence intensity [18,37]. For 
flames with an effective Lewis number less than unity, the flame 
front is governed by molecular diffusion, and perturbations lead to 
increased wrinkling through thermodiffusive instabilities. The 
increased flame surface area leads to increased turbulent burning 
velocity. To quantify this effect, a volume weighted effective Lewis 
number (Leeff) is computed for all g ¼ 1.0 fuel blends between 
v ¼ 0.3 and v ¼ 1.0 in 0.1 intervals [7]. Results are depicted in Fig. 6. 
The derivative of Leeff is observed to increase as v decreases, leading 
to larger changes in turbulent burning velocity and flame stretch 
sensitivity as hydrogen is substituted for natural gas.
Instantaneous OH-PLIF images for the M-1 case are shown in 
Fig. 7(a) alongside synchronized p0 measurements at location C2 in 
Fig. 7(b). This case corresponds to the most hydrogen prominent fuel 
mixture of the methane (M) cases. Low amplitude p0 fluctuations are 
observed. Attenuation of the OH signal is observed in the −y 
direction for each image, which is a result of laser sheet absorption 
through the probe volume. Regions of significant OH intensity are 
compact and localized around the reactant jets, indicating flame 
Fig. 5 Representative pressure time history (a) and spectral power distribution (b) and (c) from pressure 
measurements at location C2
Fig. 6 Variation of effective Lewis number with v
Fig. 7 Instantaneous OH-PLIF snapshots (a) and corresponding p0 time history (b) depicted for case M-1 
Journal of Engineering for Gas Turbines and Power                                                                                                                                      
DECEMBER 2025, Vol. 147 /

title: School of Aeronautics and Astronautics,
text:
stabilization within the shear layer (i). The highly reactive fuel 
mixture produces a thin reaction zone [38], and regions between the 
jets show a void in OH signal intensity indicating a lack of reactions 
occurring in these areas. Significant flame wrinkling is observed, 
resulting in fine scale flame structures surrounding the premixed 
jets. The scale of turbulent flame structures increases as the flow is 
advected, with larger features observed at the end of the reactant jets 
(ii). Unburned pockets of reactant are periodically shed from the 
ends of the jets, leading to local surface area increase and subsequent 
burning that is highlighted through regions of higher signal intensity 
(iii). While fluctuations in the flame surface area result in unsteady 
heat release, they are largely induced by hydrodynamic fluctuations 
of the reactant jet. The stretch sensitive fuel composition leads to 
rapid response of the flame to these disturbances [25]. The heat 
release fluctuations observed in this case are largely governed by the 
length-scales and timescales of the cascading turbulent structures 
present [28]. This wide range of timescales broadens the response of 
the flame in frequency space [26], resulting in weak coherence to the 
natural acoustic modes of the combustor.
A similar set of simultaneous OH-PLIF and p0 measurements are 
shown for the M-2 case in Fig. 8. In contrast to Fig. 7(b), p0
fluctuations as large as 10% of the mean chamber pressure are 
observed. The largest contribution to this amplitude occurs at a 
frequency consistent with the 2 L acoustic mode of the combustion 
chamber, which corresponds to a full-wave mode shape. Assuming 
small acoustic disturbances, low mean flow Mach number, and 
uniform gas composition, a positive pressure antinode at one end of a 
closed duct will result in the same antinode at the other end for this 
mode shape. Thus, p0 measurements at C2 will be closely in-phase 
with pressure fluctuations in the flame zone

title: School of Aeronautics and Astronautics,
text:
results in coherent heat release at a frequency resonant with the 
fundamental 1 L mode of the combustor.
The distinct pinch-off behavior of the M-3 case is attributed to a 
further reduction in reactivity and stretch sensitivity compared to the 
other CH4 cases. As previously noted, the largest change in Leeff
occurs between the M-2 and M-3 cases in Fig. 6. The increasing 
sensitivity of Lewis number with methane addition highlights the 
different flame responses observed as fuel composition varies.
3.2 Effect of Ammonia Addition. While the observed changes 
in unsteady heat release can be linked to underlying physical 
mechanisms governing the propagation of H2/CH4 flames, the effect 
of ammonia addition in a ternary blend is much less understood. To 
investigate the impact of ammonia addition, OH-PLIF images are 
generated for the A-2 and A-3 cases and depicted in Figs. 10 and 11, 
respectively. The distributions of OH in these cases are notably 
weaker than those of Figs. 8 and 9, which suggest a more distributed 
reaction zone with the addition of ammonia. Indeed, ammonia 
flames are typically characterized by low reactivity and diffuse heat 
release [29].
The flame response to the 2 L mode, depicted in Fig. 10(a), shows 
longitudinal fluctuations of the reactant jets consistent with the 
compression and expansion phases of the acoustic cycle. Oscil­
lations are notably less than the M-2 case depicted in Fig. 8(a), which 
is likely due to the difference in p0
RMS between the two. Despite this, 
key differences in flame structure and evolution are observed. The 
largest gradients in OH signal occur at the ends of the jets and are 
characterized by regions of significant flame corrugation, as seen in 
the uppermost jet on frames 3–6 (i). This contrasts the high intensity 
regions of Fig. 8(a) characterized by rapid burning through increases 
in flame surface area. The reduction in kinetic rates with the addition 
Fig. 9 Instantaneous OH-PLIF snapshots (a) and corresponding p0 time history (b) depicted for case M-3 
Fig. 10 Instantaneous OH-PLIF snapshots (a) and corresponding p0 time history (b) depicted for case A-2 
Journal of Engineering for Gas Turbines and Power                                                                                                                                      
DECEMBER 2025, Vol. 147 / 121018-7 
Downloaded from http://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/gasturbinespower/article-pdf/147/12/121018/7530910/gtp-25-1276.pdf by University of Cincinnati user on 31 December 2025

of ammonia results in longer timescales for preheat and consump­
tion of reactants. This increases the flame thickness and thus the 
length-scales of flame–turbulence interaction.
Figure 11 portrays several OH-PLIF snapshots with simultaneous 
p0 measurements for the A-3 case. The global response of the flame is 
similar to the results seen in Fig. 9(a), but several unique features are 
noted. During compression on frames 3–5, reactant jets near the 
walls of the combustor appear shorter than the center jet. A 
flame–vortex interaction is also observed on frame 5 (i), showing 
entrainment of reactants and enhanced burning in the surrounding 
region. The central jet at y ¼ 0, conversely, appears straighter with 
less large-scale fluctuations in the surrounding flame surface area. It 
is possible that the symmetry of the central jet leads to a relative 
balance of flame–flame interactions that results in a reduced impact 
on the local flame structure.
4 Conclusions
The impact of fuel composition on the structure and dynamics of 
lean-premixed H2/CH4 and H2/NH3/CH4 flames was investigated in 
a high-pressure multi-element combustor. Time resolved images of 
the flame response to acoustic disturbances were obtained using 
100 kHz OH-PLIF along with simultaneous measurements of 
chamber pressure fluctuations. Two unstable modes of the 
combustor were studied with and without the inclusion of ammonia, 
as well as a case of stable operation. The following conclusions are 
noted:
(1) The increase in instability magnitude for test cases with 
methane addition can be correlated to increased corrugation 
of the flame front and to commensurate heat release 
fluctuations. Variations in flame speed, stretch sensitivity, 
and thermodiffusive effects with fuel composition alter the 
timescales of unsteady heat release, allowing in-phase 
coupling with the natural acoustic modes of the combustor.
(2) Unstable cases corresponding to the 2 L mode of the 
combustor see regions of OH between reactant jets persisting 
through the expansion phase of the acoustic cycle. In contrast, 
flame pinch-off, extinction, and re-ignition are seen globally 
in cases that excite the 1 L mode.
(3) Ammonia addition results in weaker gradients in OH 
intensity, which indicates a greater dispersion of reactions 
and a corresponding increase in flame thickness. The 
resulting flame structure sees significant corrugation as a 
result.
These findings highlight the disparate chemical and mixing 
timescales of ammonia, hydrogen, and methane combustion and 
provide understanding of the resulting effects on the structure and 
dynamics of these flames in turbulent, high-pressure environments. 
For retrofit gas turbine combustor applications, it is clear that 
significant consideration of the injector geometry is necessary for 
fuel flexible operation with these fuels. However, the unique 
properties of premixed hydrogen flames could be leveraged for 
flame stability with ammonia and methane in these devices.

This work was supported by the Department of Energy University 
Turbine Systems Research (UTSR) program Grant No. DE- 
FE0032074 (program manager Andrew O’Connell). The high- 
speed imaging equipment used in this work was purchased with 
Defense University Research Instrumentation Program Grant Nos. 
FA9550-16-1-0534 and FA9550-23-1-0403 (program manager: 
Chiping Li).

The datasets generated and supporting the findings of this article 
are obtainable from the corresponding author upon reasonable 
request.
